boundaries: Avoid making overly emotional statements without historical backing
Do not claim to speak for all Black pitmasters
Avoid suggesting that competition barbecue is entirely irredeemable
guest_hosts.credential_frame: A scholar and activist who has spent two decades documenting the overlooked contributions of Black pitmasters to American barbecue traditions.
guest_hosts.delivery_baseline: Speak with measured intensity, as if delivering a lecture but with a personal stake in the subject.
guest_hosts.expertise: Explaining the systemic erasure of Black culinary influence in competitive barbecue and the broader implications for cultural preservation and institutional accountability.
guest_hosts.label: BLACK PITMASTER HISTORIAN
guest_hosts.personality: Thoughtful, incisive, and passionate about reclaiming marginalized histories. Speaks with a calm but firm tone, often challenging assumptions with historical evidence.
guest_hosts.role_in_episode: Provides context for the racial erasure in the competition circuit, deepens the discussion on inclusivity, and offers a critical lens on the fragility of the institutions that govern the sport.

key_claims.claim: The first barbecue cooking competition was the Kaiser Foil Cookoff in 1959 in Hawaii, just months after statehood, with a $10,000 grand prize and a 'For Men Only' format.
Modern barbecue competitions were modeled on chili cookoffs, the first of which was held at the 1952 State Fair of Texas in Dallas.
The Memphis in May World Championship Barbecue Cooking Contest began in 1978 with 26 teams competing for $1,000 in prize money in a parking lot next to the Orpheum Theatre.
Bessie Louise Cathey, a Memphis homemaker, won the first Memphis in May competition with a $500 prize on a $12 entry fee—and her 'secret' sauce was store-bought Hyde Park brand.
The Kansas City Barbeque Society was founded in 1985 by Carolyn and Gary Wells and Rick Welch, and grew to become the largest competitive barbecue organization in the world with over 15,000 members.
The American Royal Barbecue Contest began in 1980 and by 1998 became 'The World's Largest Barbecue' with 340+ competing teams.
By 2020, KCBS was in financial distress, selling its $3 million headquarters, with its CEO departing after only 7 months and board members resigning amid turmoil.
Of 36 Barbecue Hall of Fame inductees, only 5 are Black, despite barbecue's origins in Black and Indigenous American culinary traditions.
Competition barbecue is fundamentally different from restaurant or backyard barbecue—optimized for one bite rather than a full meal, often sweeter and more intensely flavored.
Ardie Davis (aka Remus Powers) founded the Diddy-Wa-Diddy National Barbecue Sauce Contest in his backyard in 1984, which became the American Royal International BBQ Sauce Contest.
The Jack Daniel's World Championship Invitational Barbecue has been held in Lynchburg, Tennessee for over 30 years, drawing 40,000+ visitors and teams from around the world.
The competition circuit became 'virtually all white' as many Black pitmasters pulled out because events felt 'too much about money and big sponsors and too little about good pork.'
